coredata.dc:title: Ratio-fluorescence sensor based on carbon dots and PtRu/CN nanozyme for efficient detection of melatonin in tablet
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Mengjun
Luo, Xiaojun
Jiang, Minghang
Zhang, Liyun
Zhou, Qian
Wu, Caijun
He, Yi
coredata.dc:description: The identification and quantification of melatonin (MT) are crucial for early diagnosis of disorders associated with circadian rhythm disruption. Herein, novel blue-emissive carbon dots (BCDs) were synthesized through an improved hydrothermal treatment using serine and malic acid as reductant and carbon source. The excellent optical properties of the as-obtained BCDs were used for ratiometric sensing by strategically constructing a MT sensing system integrating BCDs with C3N4 nanosheets loaded with platinum/ruthenium nanoparticles (PtRu/CN). In this system, H2O2 activated the peroxidase-like activity of PtRu/CN to generate •OH and 1O2 for oxidizing the colorless o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into yellow 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with fluorescence emission at 565 nm. Concurrently, the fluorescence emission of BCDs at 439 nm was quenched by the generated DAP via the static quenching and inner filter effect (IFE) process. However, MT rapidly scavenged the generated free radicals to reverse the ratio fluorescence signal. The developed BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2 sensing platform enabled quantitative analysis of MT at concentrations ranging from 0.06 to 600 μmol/L with a low detection limit of 23.56 nmol/L. Moreover, smartphone-based RGB sensing of MT was successfully developed for rapid visualization and portable processing. More broadly, novel insights into the preparation of carbon dots with sensitive fluorescence sensing properties were presented, promising for future considerations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Nanozyme
Ratiometric
Carbon dots
Melatonin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of melatonin in real sample
Feasibility and mechanism of MT detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis route of BCDs. (b, c) TEM images of BCDs and corresponding particle size distribution. (d) The FTIR spectrum of BCDs. (e) XPS spectra and high-resolution (f) C 1s, (g) N 1s and (h) O 1s of BCDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of BCDs (Inset: photographs of BCDs solution taken under UV light (right) and sunlight (left). (b) Color gamut of BCDs. (c) Fluorescence spectra of BCDs at varied excitation wavelengths (280–410 nm). Effects of NaCl concentration (d) and pH (e) on the fluorescence of BCDs. (f) Stability of BCDs.

(a) Synthesis process of PtRu/CN. (b) TEM image of PtRu/CN. (c) XRD pattern of PtRu/CN. (d) UV–vis spectra and photographs of different systems: TMB, H2O2+TMB, H2O2+TMB+C3N4, TMB+C3N4, H2O2+TMB+PtRu/CN, TMB+PtRu/CN. (e) The absorbance of the PtRu/CN/TMB/H2O2 system after addition of varied radical scavengers.

(a) Schematic illustration of BCDs/OPD/MT/PtRu/CN/MT-based MT sensing. (b) Fluorescence signals of varied systems: BCDs, BCDs/H2O2, BCDs/OPD, BCDs/MT, BCDs/PtRu/CN, BCDs/OPD/MT/PtRu/CN, and BCDs/OPD/MT/PtRu/CN/MT. (c) The overlap of the emission spectra of BCDs and absorbance spectra of DAP. (d) Fluorescence lifetimes of BCDs with and without DAP. (e) Fluorescence spectra of BCDs/PtRu/CN/H2O2 with a range of MT concentrations (0, 0.06, 0.4, 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400, and 600 μmol/L). (f) Negative correlation curve between the F556/F439 and the concentration of MT. (g) Fitting curve between F556/F439 and logarithm of MT concentration. (h) Chromaticity coordinates diagram for BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2 with varying MT concentration. (i) Plot of (R+G) versus MT concentration (0, 0.25, 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400, and 600 μmol/L).

(a) Relative intensities (F556/F439) of the BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2 system in response to potential interfering substances. (b) Specificity of the proposed BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2-based sensing strategy for MT analysis. MT: 0.5 mmol/L, interfering substances: 5 mmol/L. Error bars in the graph represent the standard deviation derived from three parallel experiments.
Schematic diagram of a BCDs/PtRu/CN-based ratio fluorescent sensor for MT analysis.
Detection results of MT in melatonin tablets.

head.ce:title: Ratio-fluorescence sensor based on carbon dots and PtRu/CN nanozyme for efficient detection of melatonin in tablet

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610039, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610039, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2-based fluorescence sensor for MT sensing.

A novel blue-emissive carbon dots (BCDs) peak at 439 nm were synthesized through an improved hydrothermal treatment using serine and malic acid as reductant and carbon source. Taking the advantages of splendid luminescence performance of BCDs, a sensitive and selective ratiometric sensor for monitoring melatonin (MT) was constructed by integrating BCDs and C3N4 nanosheets loaded with platinum/ruthenium nanoparticles (PtRu/CN) for the first time. In sensing strategy, H2O2 activated the peroxidase-like activity of PtRu/CN to generate •OH and 1O2 for oxidizing the colorless o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into yellow 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with fluorescence emission at 565 nm. Concurrently, the fluorescence emission of BCDs was quenched by the generated DAP via the static quenching and IFE process. However, MT rapidly scavenged the generated free radicals to reverse the ratio fluorescence signal. The developed sensor enabled quantitative analysis of MT at concentrations ranging from 0.06 to 600 μmol/L with an ultralow detection limit of 23.56 nmol/L. Smartphone-based RGB sensing of MT was successfully developed for rapid visualization and portable processing.

The identification and quantification of melatonin (MT) are crucial for early diagnosis of disorders associated with circadian rhythm disruption. Herein, novel blue-emissive carbon dots (BCDs) were synthesized through an improved hydrothermal treatment using serine and malic acid as reductant and carbon source. The excellent optical properties of the as-obtained BCDs were used for ratiometric sensing by strategically constructing a MT sensing system integrating BCDs with C3N4 nanosheets loaded with platinum/ruthenium nanoparticles (PtRu/CN). In this system, H2O2 activated the peroxidase-like activity of PtRu/CN to generate •OH and 1O2 for oxidizing the colorless o-phenylenediamine (OPD) into yellow 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with fluorescence emission at 565 nm. Concurrently, the fluorescence emission of BCDs at 439 nm was quenched by the generated DAP via the static quenching and inner filter effect (IFE) process. However, MT rapidly scavenged the generated free radicals to reverse the ratio fluorescence signal. The developed BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2 sensing platform enabled quantitative analysis of MT at concentrations ranging from 0.06 to 600 μmol/L with a low detection limit of 23.56 nmol/L. Moreover, smartphone-based RGB sensing of MT was successfully developed for rapid visualization and portable processing. More broadly, novel insights into the preparation of carbon dots with sensitive fluorescence sensing properties were presented, promising for future considerations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Melatonin is an indoleamine hormone secreted by the pineal gland of vertebrates [1], and regarded as a biological regulator of the circadian rhythm for synchronizing the physiological, metabolic, and behavioral processes according to the environmental light–dark cycle and intensity [2,3]. Melatonin can also serve as a free radical scavenger, immune enhancer, anti-inflammatory agent, and bone protector [4–6]. The daily levels of melatonin in the serum of healthy individuals range from a few pM during the daytime to 200 pM at night [7,8]. Within an appropriate range, melatonin regulates multiple physiological and neuroendocrine processes, while abnormal levels may lead to certain mental, sleep, and mood disorders. Therefore, considering the impact on the endocrine system and other metabolic processes, accurate and real-time determination of melatonin is necessary.

Various methods have so far been used for the detection and determination of melatonin, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), and electrochemical sensing [9,10]. Despite the advantages of these methods in terms of high sensitivity and accuracy, the measurement of melatonin can be operationally complex, or time-consuming. By contrast, optical detection methods for melatonin are cost-effective, sensitive, accurate, and easy to operate, making them appealing to researchers. For instance, Afshar et al. [11] developed a fluorescence sensing platform by encapsulating Zr-based MOF into the molecularly imprinted polymer (MOF@MIP) to form a sensing platform with high-specificity and sensitivity toward melatonin detection in fruit juice samples. Thus, the construction of a fluorometric sensor with high sensing performance for monitoring MT is of great significance.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanomaterials characterized by tunable optical properties, remarkable photostability, and biocompatibility [12], endowing them with potential applications in various fields like catalysis [13,14], sensing [15,16], and bioimaging [17,18]. The fluorescent CDs can be integrated with other nanomaterials to construct a sensing platform for the analysis of various target substances. For instance, Lv et al. [19] proposed a dual-mode sensing platform integrating fluorometric and colorimetric techniques based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and magnetic Fe nanoparticles for the analysis of α-glucosidase and its inhibitors. Duan et al. [20] established a ratiometric probe by integrating CDs and polyethyleneimine for quantitative analysis of morin. These studies pave the way for the construction of fluorescence sensing platforms based on CDs and nanomaterials.

Herein, blue-emissive carbon dots (BCDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal strategy using malic acid and tyrosine as reductant and carbon source. The as-obtained BCDs exhibited robust water solubility, stable fluorescence emission, and typical excitation-dependent behavior. The coupling of BCDs with C3N4 nanosheets loaded with platinum/ruthenium nanoparticles (PtRu/CN) with robust catalytic performance enabled the detection of MT with excellent sensitivity and accuracy. By recording the fluorescence alterations in BCDs (F439) and the oxidation product 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP, F556) of o-phenylenediamine (OPD), a linear relationship between the ratio fluorescence values (F565/F439) and MT concentrations was achieved (Scheme 1 ). The resulting BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2-based sensing platform exhibited a low detection limit (LOD) of 23.56 nmol/L. The RGB values at different concentrations of MT suggested a quantitative relationship between MT concentration and color for facilitated visual analysis of MT without complex equipment. In sum, foundations for integrating carbon dots and nanozyme into one sensing system were provided, promising for future high-performance analysis of MT in real samples.

Melatonin (≥98 %, M118674), chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6·6H2O), ruthenium (III) chloride trihydrate (RuCl3·3H2O), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were provided by Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). O-phenylenediamine (OPD) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were bought from China National Medicines Corporation Ltd. (Beijing, China). Alanine (Ala), arginine (Arg), histidine (His), phenylalanine (Phe), proline (Pro), threonine (Thr), glucose (Glu), fructose (Fru), galactose (Gal), maltose (Mal), and lactose (Lac) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium acetate (NaAc), acetic acid (HAc), NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, Na2CO3, Na2SO4 and NaNO3 were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All individual solutions were prepared using 18 MΩ deionized water.

Fluorescence (FL) spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra, and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) were recorded on RF-5301 fluorescence spectrometer, SHIMADZU UV-2700 UV–visible spectrophotometer, and Nicolet IS10 infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were conducted utilizing a ESCALAB Xi+ instrument (Thermo Fisher, USA), and the resulting data were analyzed using XPSPEAK 41 software. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed by the Talos F200X electron microscope. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were achieved on a Smart Lab SE (Japan Rigaku Co.). The pH values were measured using the PH-100 pro pH meter from Hangzhou, China.

The blue-emissive BCDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method. Briefly, 1.82 g of malic acid and 1.425 g of serine were dissolved in 20.0 mL of deionized water under sonication. The formed evenly dispersed mixture was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined reactor and subjected to a 10 h reaction at 240 °C. After cooling to ambient temperature naturally, the crude BCDs solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm syringe filter, followed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min and subsequent dialysis for 2 h (MWCO 1000 Da). The as-obtained light-yellow BCDs were stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments.

PtRu/CN was synthesized by a one-pot facile co-reduction strategy involving two chemical steps. Firstly, a homogeneous precursor solution of Pt4+/Ru3+ was obtained by mixing 0.33 mL of RuCl3·3H2O (38.6 mmol/L) with 1.67 mL of H2PtCl6·6H2O (38.6 mmol/L). The obtained mixture was then added dropwise to a brown C3N4 aqueous solution (1.25 mg mL−1) with a volume of 16 mL under stirring for 10 min. Afterward, 4 mL of C6H5Na3O7·2H2O (6 mg mL−1) and 20 mL of NaBH4 (1.25 mg mL−1) were sequentially introduced into the aforementioned mixture followed by stirring at room temperature for 12 h, the resultant crude product was filtered, washed, and dried to yield PtRu/CN.

The melatonin analysis was carried by introducing 50 μL of melatonin at varying concentrations into a mixture containing 50 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (0.1 mmol/L, pH 5.0), 30 μL of OPD (10 mmol/L), 30 μL of H2O2 (10 mmol/L), 50 μL of PtRu/CN (200 μg mL−1), and 290 μL of deionized water. The resulting mixture was incubated at 50 ℃ for 15 min, followed by the addition of 220 μL of phosphate buffer (pH 8.0) and 30 μL of BCDs. After standing for 1 min, the fluorescence signals of the mixtures were recorded at a fixed excitation of 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of melatonin in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of our method for the determination of melatonin in real samples was validated by analyzing melatonin tablets purchased from a local pharmacy. The tablets were first ground into powder using a mortar, followed by dissolution in ultra-pure water and ultrasound treatment for 1 h. Subsequently, the sample solution was filtered by a filter membrane to obtain a clear solution. Fixed concentrations (50, 100, and 200 μmol/L) of MT solution were then spiked into the actual samples followed by analysis according to the procedure of Section 2.4.

The blue-emissive BCDs prepared by the hydrothermal method were optimized by examining the critical parameters affecting the fluorescence properties, including the ratio of malic acid to serine (Mmalic acid:Mserine), temperature and reaction time. As shown in Fig. S1 of the Supporting Material, conditions consisting of Mmalic acid:Mserine = 1:1, 10 h, and 240 °C were selected to prepare BCDs. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and size distribution of the BCDs are presented in Fig. 1 b and c, respectively. BCDs with a spherical shape (average diameter 3.75 nm ± 0.5 nm) and uniform dispersion were recorded in the inset of Fig. 1c. A comparative analysis of malic acid, serine, and BCDs was then conducted by Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. In Fig. 1d, malic acid displayed three characteristic peaks at around 1705.12 cm−1, 1007.87 cm−1, and 3448.59 cm−1, originating from the stretching vibration of respectively C=O, C–O, and O–H [21], suggesting the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in malic acid. In the FTIR analysis of serine, two peaks were noticed at 1603.54 cm−1 and 1126.86 cm−1, assigned to the N–H and C–N stretching vibrations, respectively. The amine groups of serine and the carboxyl/hydroxyl groups of malic acid ensured the subsequent dehydration condensation and carbonization reactions to form the BCDs [22]. In the FTIR spectrum of BCDs, two distinctive bands were observed at 1720 cm−1 and 3448.59 cm−1, attributed to respectively the stretching vibration of C=O and O–H/N–H, indicating the presence of carboxyl and amine groups [23]. In addition, a relatively broad band centered at 1589.03 cm−1 was recorded, covering the C=O stretching vibration primarily ascribed to the N–H stretching vibration of free amine groups located between the amide I and II bands. Therefore, the formation of BCDs involved dehydration condensation and subsequent carbonization between carboxyl/hydroxyl and amine groups.

The elemental compositions of BCDs investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) revealed the presence of C 1s, O 1s, and, N 1s in the survey spectrum (Fig. 1e), indicating the formation of BCDs. The atomic percentages of C, N, and O elements were determined as 69.38 at. %, 4.13 at. %, and 25.97 at. %, respectively. In Fig. 1f, four peaks at 284.49 eV, 285.05 eV, 285.82 eV, and 288.46 eV were observed in the C 1s spectrum, assigned to the binding energies of −C=C, −C–N, −C–O, and −C=O, respectively [24]. The N 1 s spectrum in Fig. 1g showed the presence of C–N (400.65 eV) and H–N (399.90 eV), while the O1s spectrum (Fig. 1h) confirmed the existence of C–O (531.60 eV), C=O (532.31 eV), and O–H (533.23 eV). The above results confirmed the successful preparation of BCDs with surfaces rich in amine and carboxyl functional groups.

The optical properties of BCDs were systematically investigated. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of BCDs (Fig. 2 a), the broad absorption band at 275 nm can be ascribed to the π-π* transition of C=C and n-π* transition of C=O [25,26]. The aqueous solution of BCDs displayed vibrant blue fluorescence under a 365 nm handheld irradiation lamp (inset of Fig. 2a). The excitation at 356 nm produced a vibrant emission peak at 432 nm, boasting a high quantum yield (QY) of 16.55 %, consistent with the results from the Commission International de l’Éclairage [27] of (0.159, 0.017) (Fig. 2b). Similar to previously reported fluorescent nanoparticles [28], the BCDs also exhibited an excitation-dependent behavior. As illustrated in Fig. 2c, the BCDs confirmed excitation-dependent luminescent characteristics, with the maximum shift in the fluorescence peak from 395 to 467 nm (inset of Fig. 2c), suggesting the BCDs can downshift the higher-energy UV light to lower-energy visible light. The stability tests of BCDs were depicted in Fig. 2d. The stable fluorescence signal output of BCDs in the presence of elevated sodium chloride concentrations indicated favorable tolerance to high ionic strength. In Fig. 2e, the fluorescence intensity exhibited minimal variation with pH from 3.0 to 9.0, implying favorable pH stability of BCDs. Additionally, the modest decrease in fluorescence (3.7 %) after storage for 24 days (Fig. 2f) suggested excellent stability of BCDs. These findings collectively indicated BCDs possessing satisfactory stability, useful for applications in sensing.

The preparation procedure of PtRu/CN is displayed in Fig. 3 a. After the room temperature co-reduction strategy, PtRu/CN was prepared from CN and mixed Pt4+/Ru3+ as the precursors. The TEM image in Fig. 3b revealed a nanosheet structure of PtRu/CN. The XRD data in Fig. 3c illustrated the presence of characteristic peaks of PtRu/CN at (110), (200), (220), and (311), closely aligning with the standard card (JCPDS No. 04-0802) [29,30]. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, H2O2/TMB/PtRu/CN exhibited a substantial increase in absorbance intensity when compared to TMB, H2O2/TMB, H2O2/TMB/C3N4, TMB/C3N4, and TMB/PtRu/CN system, indicating the excellent peroxidase-mimicking activity of PtRu/CN. Furthermore, the radical scavenging experiments confirmed the specific ROS generated within the PtRu/CN/H2O2 system. As shown in Fig. 3e, the absorbance of the PtRu/CN/H2O2 system upon introducing isopropanol (IPA) remained nearly constant, while a pronounced decrease was observed after adding p-BQ and NaN3, suggesting •OH and 1O2 as active intermediates in the PtRu/CN/H2O2 system. Therefore, the developed PtRu/CN exhibited excellent catalytic activity that can be used as a reliable substitute for HRP in biosensing applications. The reusability experiment demonstrated that PtRu/CN maintained satisfactory activity following five repeated measurements, implying its acceptable reusability (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and mechanism of MT detection
ce:section.ce:para: The acting mechanism of the proposed ratiometric fluorescence strategy for the detection of MT is displayed in Fig. 4 b. Note that the influence of H2O2, OPD, MT, and PtRu/CN on the fluorescence of BCDs can be neglected. In the presence of PtRu/CN, species of H2O2 were catalytically oxidized to generate •OH and 1O2, which then oxidized OPD into DAP to yield a fluorescence emission peak at 556 nm. However, in the absence of PtRu/CN, the influence of MT on the BCDs/H2O2/OPD system was slight (Fig. S3), indicating that the reaction between H2O2 and melatonin can be considered negligible. Moreover, the addition of MT with the scavenging ability of free radicals to the BCDs/H2O2/OPD/PtRu/CN system disrupted the catalytic oxidation of OPD to DAP, leading to the fluorescence recovery of BCDs. Therefore, the designed BCDs/H2O2/OPD/PtRu/CN-based ratio fluorescence system was successful for the quantitation of MT based on the change in fluorescence intensity ratio F556/F439.

The underlying mechanism of DAP-regulated fluorescence quenching of BCDs was further explored. As shown in Fig. S4a, a new peak was generated in the UV–vis absorption spectra of BCDs/DAP, indicating that the complex was formed between the quencher DAP and BCDs. A significant overlap was observed between the emission band of BCDs and the absorption spectrum of DAP (Fig. 4c), suggesting the potential presence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or IFE between BCDs and DAP. The IFE is an effective fluorescence quenching mechanism, stemming from the overlap between the absorption spectrum of the absorbing species in the analytical system and the fluorescence emission spectrum or excitation spectrum of the fluorophore, where the absorbing species absorbs the emitted and/or excitation light [31]. To validate the quenching mechanism in the sensing strategy, Stern–Volmer (SV) analysis was conducted. The equation used for this analysis is expressed as follows [32]:
F 0 /
F
=
1
+ kq τ 0
Q
=
1
+ Ksv
Q
;
F
0 /
F
= τ 0 / τ

Kq denotes the quenching constant, [Q] represents the concentration of DAP, and KSV signifies the SV quenching coefficient. F and F0​ stand for the FL intensities of BCDs with or without DAP, while τ and τ0 correspond to the lifetimes of BCDs with or without DAP, respectively. As shown in Fig. S4b, the signal intensity ratio F0/F demonstrated excellent linearity with DAP concentration ranging from 0 to 1 μmol/L. The Ksv values decreased as the temperature increased, indicating that the stability of the ground-state complex declined with rising temperature, thereby reducing the effect of static quenching [33]. The results of the SV analysis confirmed the presence of a static quenching mechanism within the BCDs/PDA system. Additionally, upon the addition of DAP, the fluorescence decay lifetime of BCDs reduced from 5.65 ns to 5.61 ns (Fig. 4d). These findings confirmed that PDA can quench the fluorescence of BCDs through both static quenching and IFE process.

Appropriate sensing conditions would be a crucial prerequisite for the sensitive determination of MT of the BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2-based fluorescent sensors. Thus, the impacts of testing conditions were studied at various temperatures, pH values, and PtRu/CN concentrations. From Fig. S5 and S6, the optimal conditions for sensor construction were determined as temperature of 50 ℃, pH of 5.0, PtRu/CN concentration of 20 μmol/L, OPD concentration of 0.6 mmol/L, reaction time of 15 min, H2O2 concentration of 0.6 mmol/L, and equilibrium time of l min. The sensing performances of the BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2-based sensor evaluated under these optimal experimental conditions are illustrated in Fig. 4e. The fluorescence signal exhibited a gradual increase as a function of MT concentration from 0 to 600 μmol/L. A linear correlation was observed between the logarithm of MT concentration and the fluorescent ratio F556/F439 within the concentration range of 0.06 to 600 μmol/L. The derived linear equation was determined as: F556/F439 = −0.34lg [MT] + 1.10 (Fig. 4f and g), with a correlation coefficient of 0.99 and a LOD value of 23.56 nmol/L (signal/noise = 3 [34]). The reproducibility of the method was evaluated at a concentration of 600 μmol/L melatonin. The RSDs for the ratiometric fluorescence assay were consistently below 6.72 %, demonstrating its good reproducibility. The discernible shift in fluorescent color was confirmed through International Commission on Illumination (CIE 1931) chromaticity (Fig. 4h). The continuous movement of the CIE coordinates of the BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2 system from chartreuse (0.286, 0.355) to light-blue (0.1693, 0.144) as a function of MT concentration from 0 to 600 μmol/L satisfied the prerequisite for visual analysis of MT. In the inset of Fig. 4i, noticeable color-gradient variations from light-yellow to deep-yellow were observed with the increase in MT concentration. The RGB values of color intensity employed for the visual quantitative analysis of MT are depicted in Fig. 4i. Obviously, the (R+G) model exhibited a well-defined linear correlation within the MT concentration range of 0.25–600 μmol/L, which can be expressed by the linear regression equation: R+G = −80.56lg [MT]+292.78 (R2 = 0.984). The LOD of MT was calculated as 84.61 nmol/L. The results are comparable to the early reported method for MT analysis (Table S1), demonstrating that our sensing strategy provided gratifying sensitivity for MT.

Selectivity and anti-interference capability of the BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2-based sensor were also evaluated as essential characteristics [34,35]. As displayed in Fig. 5 a, the presence of MT resulted in a significantly reduced fluorescence intensity ratio of F556/F439 while the addition of a series of interfering substances led to no visible signal quenching. Thus, the BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2-based sensor was selective against interfering substances. The anti-interference capability of the sensing system evaluated by introducing potential interferences into an analytical system containing MT (Fig. 5b) confirmed a consistent response of the BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2-based sensing platform to MT in the presence of these interferents, indicating satisfactory interference ability and exceptional specificity of the sensor toward MT analysis.

The practicability of the BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2-based sensing platform was validated in real samples of melatonin tablets purchased from a local pharmacy. As illustrated in Table 1 , the fluorescence analysis results in melatonin tablets exhibited recoveries from 94.80 % to 101.10 %, with RSD consistently below 3.10 %. Therefore, the fluorescent system based on BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2 was suitable for the quantitative analysis of MT in complex environments.

The blue-emissive BCDs were successfully synthesized through an improved hydrothermal treatment using serine and malic acid as reductant and carbon source. The remarkable optical properties of BCDs were used to construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for MT analysis by integrating BCDs with PtRu/CN. The resulting BCDs/PtRu/CN/OPD/H2O2-based sensor for MT analysis exhibited praiseworthy colorimetric sensing characteristics toward MT analysis, including a low detection limit (23.56 nmol/L) and wide calibration range (0.06–600 μmol/L). Furthermore, smartphone-based RGB analysis of MT was achieved for visual and portable sensing. In sum, a paradigm for synergistically integrating carbon dots and nanozyme to construct a ratio fluorescence sensor was provided for the practical detection of MT in real samples with high analytical performance. Future efforts will focus on designing a more sensitive and stable sensing system and expanding its practical applications in biology and clinical diagnostics.

Mengjun Wang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Xiaojun Luo: Formal analysis, Data curation. Minghang Jiang: Methodology, Conceptualization. Liyun Zhang: Resources. Qian Zhou: Software. Caijun Wu: Investigation. Yi He: Supervision, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Xihua University Talent Initiation Funding Programs (Z222053).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124699.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary material to this article:
